summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the 1994 book 'Showstopper!' about the creation of Windows NT and its legendary architect David Cutler. He reflects on NT's lasting significance, noting that the world has consolidated into just Unix and NT camps, with no new operating systems being developed from scratch. The post examines key lessons from NT's development: dogfooding, long-term R&D planning, prioritizing people over process, and the importance of architectural vision. Atwood concludes by questioning whether we'll ever see another ground-up operating system in our lifetimes, given that both Unix and NT have proven remarkably durable.
key_points: The computing world has devolved into just Unix and NT camps - nobody is writing new operating systems anymore, and even Apple abandoned their Copland project for Unix-based OS X
Microsoft's success with NT came from hiring smart people and letting them figure things out, with shockingly little formal process for such a complex project

Cutler's insistence on portability over x86 optimization, despite intense pressure, proved his architectural foresight - the NT kernel survived and evolved even after all non-x86 versions were discontinued
Large corporations need to plan ten years out in R&D - NT took from 1989 to Windows 2000 to become truly viable on the desktop
If you can't explain your technical vision in practical, meaningful terms, you're probably full of crap - as proven by Cairo's failure
money_quotes: Nobody is writing new operating systems any more. The world has devolved into UNIX and NT camps exclusively.
Unix is like Cutler's lifelong foe. It's like his Moriarty. He thinks Unix is a junk operating system designed by a committee of Ph.D.s.
Microsoft didn't really manage much at all: they just chose to build the company with the smartest people they could find and let them figure it out.
If you can't explain what you plan to do in practical, meaningful terms – you're probably full of crap.
Ten years from now we'll be designing another system, and everyone will be sitting around bemoaning that it will have to be compatible with NT. That will happen.
I think it's possible we may not see another 'from the ground up' OS developed in our lifetimes.
key_insight: The era of building new operating systems from scratch may be permanently over, as both Unix and NT have proven durable enough to evolve rather than be replaced.
